info.parenttitle: B3.2 Structural systems application and selection (HL only)

markdown:
## Forces in Product Structures


<definition term="Structural System">The **parts of a product** that **resist loads**, such as the **shell, framework, or solid structure**, helping it maintain **strength and stability**.</definition>

### Types of Forces Acting on Structures


#### Tension


<definition term="Tension (force)">A **pulling or stretching force** that acts to **extend** a material.</definition>

![A rope in a tug of war is under tension (forbes)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/f853b38330379c643b8952c0168f02e3b1779cb3-1350x900.png)

#### Compression


<definition term="Compression (force)">A **force that squeezes** a material or causes it to **shorten**.</definition>

![The legs of a chair under compression when someone sits (pngtree)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/57ce790c276dfefc8752fa86d0ff3a0d15ac99b2-2500x2500.png)

#### Shear


<definition term="Shear (force)">Two **equal and opposite forces** acting on an object that are **not aligned**, creating a **cutting effect**.</definition>

![Scissors cutting paper is example of shear force (smithsonian)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/f1963f541435d92640410868e52a4189839d8d92-1280x960.png)

#### Torsion


<definition term="Torsion (force)">A twisting force.</definition>

![A screwdriver in use is an example of torsion](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/7c767df09445fe02cd851f90cb17e93efc26591d-626x399.png)

#### Bending


<definition term="Bending (force)">A **force that causes a material to curve**, involving both **tension** (on one side) and **compression** (on the opposite side).</definition>

![A bridge beam under weight (britannica)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3bc2cba733cb22058463ecea291c824df5db1f1f-1600x770.png)

<callout type="note">Understanding these forces is crucial for analyzing how products **withstand stress** and **maintain their integrity**.</callout>

### Strengthening Existing Structures


1. **Ribs and Fillets**: Add internal ridges or curves to distribute force.
2. **Triangulation**: Use triangular shapes to prevent distortion (common in trusses and bike frames).
3. **Bracing and Cross-Beams**: Add angled supports to resist bending or twisting.
4. **Folded or Corrugated Surfaces**: Used in cardboard, sheet metal, etc. for increased strength.
5. **Material Choice**: Select materials with suitable strength-to-weight ratios.

![Triangulation used in bike frames (somafab)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1cfccf0d603327e5ea53c4ab663cde8c66349fd4-1162x737.png)

<callout type="warning">* Don't assume that adding more material always strengthens a structure. 
* It's essential to consider how the material is **distributed** and how it **interacts** with forces.</callout>
